1: ʿad māṯay ʾattāh miṯʾabbēl ʾel šāʾûl — "How long will you mourn (ʾābal) for Saul?" The question is not reproach but a call to transition: grief is appropriate, but YHWH has moved on (mēʾastîhû, perfect — the rejection is complete and in the past). mallēʾ qarnəḵā šāmen — "fill your horn with oil" — the horn of anointing oil is the instrument of covenant installation; the command to fill it is the command to begin the new administration. bāḥartî bānāyw — "I have chosen among his sons" — bāḥar (H977, to choose/elect) is the covenant-election verb used of Israel (Deut 7:6) and of David consistently in later texts (Ps 89:3).
2: ʾêḵ ʾēlēḵ wəšāmaʿ šāʾûl wəhăraḡānî — "How can I go? If Saul hears (šāmaʿ), he will kill me." Samuel's fear activates the šāmaʿ vocabulary again: Saul's hearing is dangerous. YHWH's answer is a concealment strategy — take a sacrifice. The practical accommodation of prophetic mission to political danger is notable: YHWH does not override the danger but provides a cover.
3: ûmāšaḥtāh lî ʾēt ʾăšer ʾōmar ʾēlêḵā — "you are to anoint for me (lî) the one I indicate to you." The emphatic lî (for me / on my behalf) underscores that the anointing is YHWH's act performed through Samuel. The verb ʾāmar (to say/indicate) will be enacted in v12 — YHWH's speech-act identifies the individual.
4: wayyeḥerdû ziqnê hāʿîr liqrāʾtô — "the elders of the town came trembling (ḥārad, H2729) to meet him." Ḥārad denotes trembling before what is awesome or dangerous — used of theophany encounters (Exod 19:16) and of the fear provoked by unexpected authority. Samuel's arrival provokes the same awe as a divine appearance; his prophetic office carries that weight.
5: hitqaddəšû ûḇāʾtem ʾittî bazzāḇaḥ — "purify yourselves and come with me to the sacrifice." Qāddēš (Hithpael, to consecrate/purify oneself) is the ritual preparation for holy encounter — washing garments and abstaining (cf. Exod 19:10, 14). Samuel "consecrated" (wayyəqaddēš) Jesse and his sons specifically — a particular preparation beyond the communal purification, suggesting the sons are entering an especially sacred role.
6: wayyōʾmer šəmûʾēl ʾak nəged YHWH məšîḥô — "Samuel thought: surely YHWH's anointed stands before him." Nāḡad (Niphal, H5048 — stand before, in the presence of) — Samuel's internal judgment uses "before him" (nəgdô) as if YHWH is already indicating Eliab. The assumption is based on physical stature — the same error that originally made Saul plausible (9:2). The word māšîaḥ (H4899, anointed one) here is used prospectively for whoever YHWH will designate; Samuel's pre-emptive use of it for Eliab is immediately corrected.
7:
lēḇ (H3820) — the chapter's central verse: kî lōʾ ʾăšer yirʾeh hāʾādām kî hāʾādām yirʾeh lāʿênāyim waYHWH yirʾeh lallēḇ — "for man sees what is visible to the eyes, but YHWH sees the heart (lēḇ)." The antithesis is structural: hāʾādām/YHWH, ʿênāyim (eyes, external) / lēḇ (heart, internal). Lēḇ in Hebrew psychology encompasses intellect, will, and moral orientation — not just emotion. YHWH's perception penetrates to the seat of character; human sight is limited to the external. The principle governs both David's selection and the entire theology of covenantal fidelity: what qualifies for election is not external advantage but the orientation of the inner self toward YHWH.

wayqahàr (Qal imperfect with waw: "and the LORD looked at") — the verb rāʾāh (to see) is used for both sides, but YHWH's seeing penetrates where human seeing cannot. The verse also indirectly critiques Saul's appointment logic: Israel asked for a king who looked the part; YHWH now explicitly overrides that criterion.
8: wayqarāʾ yiššay ʾel ʾăḇînādāḇ — Jesse presents seven sons in sequence. The verbal pattern is formulaic: each son passes before Samuel, Samuel says "the LORD has not chosen this one" (lōʾ ḇāḥar YHWH bāzeh), and the sequence continues. The repetition enacts the progressive elimination; each use of bāḥar (choose) anticipates the positive use in v12.
9: The same formula as v8 — šammāh (Shammah) passes, and lōʾ ḇāḥar YHWH bāzeh. The sequential rejection of Jesse's sons by name emphasizes that YHWH's election is not predictable by birth order, physical presence, or family standing — the first seven are each individually considered and passed over.
10: šibʿāh ḇānāyw — "seven of his sons." Seven is the number of completion; that all seven pass before Samuel without divine selection sets up the reversal: the anointed is outside the expected frame entirely, absent from the formal presentation, tending sheep.
11: haqqāṭān — "the youngest" (qāṭān, H6996 — same word used in 15:17 of Saul's original self-assessment: "when you were small in your own eyes"). The inversion is pointed: Saul was small and was made great; David is literally the smallest/youngest and will be chosen. wəhinnēh rōʿeh baṣṣōʾn — "he is tending the sheep (rāʿāh)" — the shepherd-image here is biographical; it will become the dominant metaphor for David's kingship (Ps 78:70-72; 2 Sam 5:2).
12: ʾadmōnî ʿim yəpê ʿênāyim wəṭôḇ rōʾî — "ruddy, with beautiful eyes and handsome in appearance." ʾAdmōnî (H132, ruddy/reddish) is mentioned again in 17:42. The physical description comes after the theological principle of v7 — the narrator is not contradicting v7 but signaling that David's appearance is secondary to the divine word that follows. wayōʾmer YHWH qûm mošḥēhû kî zeh hûʾ — "YHWH said: Rise and anoint him, for this is the one" (zeh hûʾ). The divine word is monosyllabic and definitive: "this — he." No explanation of the inner qualities that qualified him; the election is pure divine prerogative.
13: rûaḥ YHWH + ṣālaḥ — "the Spirit of the LORD came powerfully upon (ṣālaḥ, H6743) David from that day onward." Ṣālaḥ denotes effective, sudden action — the same root was used of the rûaḥ coming upon Saul (10:6, 10) and Samson (Judg 14:6, 19; 15:14). The force is invasive and transformative. The phrase mēhayyôm hahûʾ wāmāʿlāh (from that day onward) marks a permanent installation, not a transient experience — contrasted with v14 where the rûaḥ "departed (sûr) from Saul." Māšaḥ bəqéreḇ ʾeḥāyw — "anointed in the midst of his brothers" — the private ceremony before witnesses; public installation comes later (2 Sam 2:4; 5:3).
14: sûr / rûaḥ rāʿāh — "the Spirit of the LORD departed (sûr, H5493) from Saul, and an evil/harmful spirit from the LORD tormented him." Sûr is the verb of departure/removal — the same root used when the ark was removed (4:21: gālāh kāḇôd, glory departed). The divine empowerment that Saul received at 10:10 is formally withdrawn. Rûaḥ rāʿāh mēʾēt YHWH — "a harmful spirit from the LORD" — the text does not identify this as a demonic being in later NT taxonomy; it is an affliction that YHWH permits/sends as part of the judicial withdrawal of protective presence. The juxtaposition of vv13-14 is deliberate: the rûaḥ moves from Saul to David in the same narrative moment.
15: Saul's servants explain the situation using the same vocabulary: rûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm rāʿāh mǝḇaʿǝtteḵā — "an evil spirit from God is troubling you" (bāʿat, H1204, to terrify/trouble). The servants recognize a theological reality — the spirit is from God — while seeking a practical remedy in music. The therapeutic use of music connects to the tradition of prophetic music (10:5) and the Psalms.
16: yôdēaʿ mənaggen — "skilled/knowing in playing" (yādaʿ + nāgan). Yādaʿ (H3045, to know) with a skill denotes professional competence — the person who "knows how" to do something. Nāgan (H5059, to play a stringed instrument) — the lyre/kinnor. The servants propose a musical-therapeutic intervention; the logic connects physical performance to spiritual relief.
17: rəʾû nāʾ lî ʾîš mêṭîḇ lənaggen — "find me a man who plays well" (literally "who does good in playing" — mêṭîḇ from ṭôḇ). The word ṭôḇ reappears: the man who is ṭôḇ at music will enter where the man the people wanted (tall, impressive) failed.
18: rāʾîtî bēn lǝyiššay bêt hallaḥmî yōdēaʿ nāḡēn — "I know of a son of Jesse the Bethlehemite, skilled in playing." The servant's description of David is striking in its fullness: skilled in music, brave (gibbôr ḥayil), man of war (ʾîš milḥāmāh), prudent in speech (nāḇôn dāḇār), handsome (ʾîš tōʾar) — and above all, waYHWH ʿimmô — "the LORD is with him." The climactic phrase "YHWH is with him" is the theological summary that will recur throughout the David narrative (18:12, 14, 28). In contrast to the rûaḥ having departed from Saul, David's defining characteristic is divine presence.
19: šilḥû ʾēlay ʾet dāwid binkā hārōʿeh baṣṣōʾn — "send me your son David, who tends the sheep." The royal summons still identifies David by his occupation — shepherd. The king who will replace Saul enters his orbit as a servant.
20: Jesse's gift: bread, wine, a young goat — the standard hospitality offering for a patron. The donkey as carrier signals the gift's weight and distance. David goes with the gift — as servant delivering tribute, not as heir presenting credentials.
21: wayʾahāḇēhû šāʾûl məʾōd — "Saul loved him greatly (ʾāhab + məʾōd)." The intensity of the affection is recorded; it will intensify into lethal hatred. wayhî lô nōśēʾ kēlîm — "and became his armor-bearer." The armor-bearer role (nōśēʾ kēlîm) is one of high personal trust — the man who carries and hands the weapons of the king in battle.
22: yaqōm nāʾ dāwid lǝpānay — "let David remain in my service" (literally "stand before me" — ʿāmad lǝpānāyw, the idiom of court attendance). Saul requests David specifically, and Jesse complies. The servant of Saul is the chosen of YHWH; the narrative tension of the next fifteen chapters is embedded in this single verse.
23: kəšeḇet rûaḥ hāʾĕlōhîm ʾel šāʾûl ... lāqaḥ dāwid ʾet hakkinnôr wəniggēn bəyādô — "whenever the harmful spirit from God came upon Saul, David took the lyre and played." The temporal clause kəšeḇet (whenever/each time) marks a recurring pattern. Kinnôr (H3658, lyre/harp) is the instrument associated with worship and praise throughout the psalmic tradition — David's instrument. The therapeutic sequence: David plays → Saul is relieved → the harmful spirit departs. The verse closes ch16 by showing David already functioning as the agent of Saul's relief — the anointed king ministering to his predecessor who does not yet know who David truly is.
